entities.name: sphoṭakāla
entities.cite: the initial articulated form is what it is

entities.name: śabdasantāna
entities.cite: the after-train, waxes and wanes (pracaya-apacaya) with the force of utterance

entities.name: prakṛta
entities.cite: the structure of prakrta versus vaikrta (1.76-77) reappears inside pure acoustics

entities.name: vaikṛta
entities.cite: the structure of prakrta versus vaikrta (1.76-77) reappears inside pure acoustics

axioms.name: na_bhidyate
axioms.cite: that first event's time 'does not differ' with loudness — alpa or mahat, whispered or bellowed

axioms.name: pracaya_apacaya_atmaka
axioms.cite: the after-train, waxes and wanes (pracaya-apacaya) with the force of utterance
even theorists who deny the transcendent word are driven to split the invariant from the variable within speech
the structure of prakrta versus vaikrta (1.76-77) reappears inside pure acoustics

denials.name: pracaya_apacaya_atmaka
denials.cite: that first event's time 'does not differ' with loudness — alpa or mahat, whispered or bellowed

denials.name: na_bhidyate
denials.cite: the after-train, waxes and wanes (pracaya-apacaya) with the force of utterance

claims.name: na_bhidyate
claims.cite: the time of the sphota does not differ

claims.name: pracaya_apacaya_atmaka
claims.cite: has the nature of increase and decrease

rejected_readings.label: reversed_invariance
rejected_readings.rendering: The time of the sphota increases and decreases with the loudness of the utterance, while the after-train remains constant.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the verse's explicit assertion: the sphota-kala does not differ (is invariant), while the sabda-santana varies. This misreads which acoustic phenomenon carries the invariance structure.

rejected_readings.label: collapse_distinction
rejected_readings.rendering: The sphota and the subsequent train of sounds are the same acoustic phenomenon, subject to the same variation.
rejected_readings.why: Ignores the critical distinction between the invariant first-produced sphota (prakrta) and the variable propagated train (vaikrta). The verse carefully separates what does and does not differ with loudness.

rejected_readings.label: propagation_invariant
rejected_readings.rendering: Both the sphota-kala and the subsequent train are invariant because both derive from the same articulation.
rejected_readings.why: Incorrectly generalizes the sphota-kala's invariance to the sabda-santana. The commentary is explicit: loudness is a property of propagation, not articulation. The after-train must vary with utterance force.

notes: The verse reports the acoustic reductionist view from 1.102, where sphota is the first produced sound (sphoṭakāla) and sabdasantāna is the propagated echo. The contract captures the key invariant structure: prakṛta (invariant articulation) splits from vaikṛta (variable propagation) within pure acoustics. Strain: the contested note flags that whether 1.103 voices Bhartrhari's own position or continues reporting the rival view of 1.102 is ambiguous in the tradition.
